costs: Statistical reconciliation and decomposition research requires sustained analytic investment and access to administrative microdata
Risk of political sensitivity around ethnically disaggregated data collection given historical avoidance (F-live-legal_and_governance-10)
content.equity_impact: Directly enables detection of Roma misclassification and county-level inequities that are currently obscured by inconsistent reporting, a precondition for any equity-targeted remedy, though data collection itself does not redistribute resources
expected_benefits: Enables evidence-based targeting of subsequent capacity, finance, or gatekeeping reforms rather than acting on contested or unaudited figures
Provides the EU Commission and ECtHR monitoring bodies with the disaggregated ethnic and regional data currently missing, potentially unblocking infringement resolution
content.goal: Establish a single, harmonized, publicly auditable statistical series and monitoring framework that disentangles prevalence change from diagnostic-practice, access, and incentive effects across SNI, BTMN, and adjacent categories

implementation_steps.action: Publish a single reconciled time series with explicit definitional notes bridging pre- and post-2018/2020 rule changes
implementation_steps.actor: KSH and Ministry responsible for education statistics

implementation_steps.action: Conduct decomposition analysis using county-level variation and policy-timing discontinuities to estimate relative contribution of drivers
Establish an annual public monitoring report disaggregating SNI/BTMN trends by category, county, gender, and cohort

mechanisms: Reconciliation of divergent existing series (e.g., 92,800→97,300 vs. 112,060 vs. 69,000 figures using different bases) into one definitionally consistent longitudinal dataset, as the current fragmentation is documented across multiple independent findings

Decomposition analysis separating the contribution of school-readiness rule changes, category redefinition (e.g., BTMN grading-exemption reform), and diagnostic-practice shifts from true prevalence change, using natural policy variation such as the 2018 and 2020 rule changes as identification points
risks: Without political commitment to act on findings, reconciled data alone may not change outcomes
Data reconciliation could reveal politically inconvenient patterns (e.g., incentive-driven over-identification) that face resistance to publication
content.title: Build a reconciled national SEN measurement and monitoring architecture
